title: Chapter 5: Full Invisibility
content: <!-- Speed: Explosion | D5+D11 -->
The grocery bag ripped as she shoved through the double doors. Apples rolled across the sidewalk. She didn't stop to pick them up.
No alarm. No shout. No heavy footsteps behind her.

Wren stood on the pavement outside Metro Fresh, a paper bag clutched against her chest, her heart slamming so hard she could taste copper at the back of her throat. The security guard — built like a vending machine, earpiece coiled into his collar — turned his head in a slow sweep of the entrance.
His gaze passed over her like sunlight through clear glass.
She was standing three feet away. He looked right through the space she occupied and saw nothing worth stopping for.

A laugh bubbled out of her — ugly, fractured, the kind of sound that belongs in a padded room. She clamped her hand over her mouth. A woman with a stroller gave the noise a quick glance, then looked away, searching for the source, failing to find one.
Wren picked up one of the fallen apples. Rubbed it on her hoodie. Bit into it.
The juice was too sweet. She chewed with her mouth open, standing in the middle of the sidewalk, a grocery thief who wasn't even a blur on someone's peripheral radar.
She walked.

Not the skittering, shoulder-hunched walk she'd practiced for twenty-six years. Not the sidewalk-dodge, the make-yourself-small, the sorry-sorry-sorry internal loop. She walked like the city was a house she owned.

Down Lex, across 52nd, past the dry cleaner where she'd once dropped off a coat and the man behind the counter had said "You sure about this one, sweetheart?" with a smile that made her skin crawl for days.
He wasn't there. The shop was dark. But the memory was, and she walked through it like smoke.

A couple argued on a stoop, the woman's voice cracking on a name. Wren paused. She could hear everything. The man's whispered reply. The woman's jagged breath. Private grief, laid bare for anyone — for no one — to witness.
She moved on.

The city was an open wound and she was the sterile air inside it. Every window was a lit stage. A man on the fourth floor of a walk-up practiced guitar scales, his fingers slow and reverent. A woman in a bathrobe stood at a kitchen counter, eating cereal straight from the box, staring at nothing. A child pressed a drawing against window glass — a cat with too many legs — and waved at the street below.
Wren waved back.
The child couldn't see her.
She pushed through the revolving door of the Harrington Grand without slowing down.

The lobby was cream marble and stale lily perfume. A crystal chandelier the size of a car hung overhead. Bellhops in pressed uniforms stood at careful attention. A woman in a fur wrap argued with the concierge in French, her bracelets chiming with each gesture.
Wren walked to the center of the lobby and stopped.
She stood on the inlaid medallion in the floor — some crest, some founding family, some dead rich man's signature on a building he'd never set foot in twice. She stood on it and she breathed.
Nobody approached her.
A bellhop walked past, close enough that his sleeve brushed her hoodie. He didn't flinch. Didn't register the contact. Probably felt a draft, nothing more.
She stood there for eleven minutes. She counted.

The concierge fielded three phone calls. Two couples checked in. A man in a stained trench coat asked for the bathroom and was firmly redirected outside. Staff moved around Wren like water around a stone — not avoiding her, because you can't avoid something that isn't there.
She sat down on a velvet settee. Stretched her legs out. Tipped her head back against the tufted silk.
The ceiling was painted with clouds. Fat, stupid, cheerful clouds. The kind she drew in the margins of her sketchbook when the anxiety got too loud and her hand needed somewhere to go.

She pulled her sketchbook from her bag. Flipped to a blank page. Drew the chandelier. Quick, aggressive strokes — the angles, the weight of it, the way light fractured through each crystal like it couldn't decide what color to be.
A hotel employee walked past and adjusted a throw pillow two feet from her head.
Wren kept drawing.

The drug was called ANX-00. Dr. Vance had said take one daily. She'd taken two now, the second one dry-swallowed on a park bench at dusk, the pill bitter and granular on her tongue before it went down.
She'd taken it the way you reach for a hand in the dark. Deliberate. Desperate.
The first pill had been an accident of timing — her anxiety so loud she'd choked it down before she could think. The second was a choice.
She wanted more of this.

Not the invisibility — well, yes, the invisibility — but the quiet that came with it. The constant engine of how-am-I-being-perceived, am-I-taking-up-too-much-space, is-that-person-looking-at-me had shut off like someone cut a power line. The silence inside her skull was enormous. Cathedral-quiet. Space-quiet.
She could hear herself think for the first time in twenty-six years.
The thoughts were messy and sharp and half-formed. But they were hers. Not reactive. Not defensive. Just — hers.
She sat in the Harrington Grand until midnight, sketching the lobby, the people, the clouds on the ceiling, until her hand cramped and the graphite was a silver smear across the heel of her palm.
Then she walked out through the revolving door, into the cold, into the city that couldn't see her.
The wind hit her face. It smelled like exhaust and roasted nuts and rain that hadn't fallen yet.
She spread her arms wide on the sidewalk. Tilted her face up.
Taxi headlights blurred through her — through the space where she stood.
A man in a suit barreled past, his shoulder passing through the inch between hers and the air.
She laughed again. Not ugly this time. Bright. Startled.
The city didn't know she was here.
The city was hers.
Home. Her apartment was a studio above a laundromat on 9th. The radiator clanked. The window looked onto a brick wall. She'd lived there for four years and the super still called her "the quiet one."
She set the grocery bag on the counter. The apples she'd saved were bruised. She didn't care.

She opened her laptop. The screen flickered — her face didn't unlock it. She typed her password. It worked. The browser loaded. Her email was still there. Her bank was still there. The digital world, at least, still knew she existed.
For now.
She pulled the pill bottle from her jacket pocket. White plastic. Child-proof cap. Orange label with blocky text: ANX-00 — 30 CAPSULES — TAKE ONE DAILY AS DIRECTED.
She counted. Twenty-eight left.
Twenty-eight days of this, if she followed the rules. Fourteen if she kept taking two.
She could stretch it. She could make it last.
Or she could live like this — really live, fully, for the first time — while it lasted.
She set the bottle on the nightstand. Lay down on her bed without removing her shoes. Stared at the ceiling. Not clouds. Just cracks and a water stain shaped like a continent nobody would ever visit.
Her phone buzzed. A text from her mother, two days old, unanswered: *Honey are you okay? You haven't called.* Below it, a follow-up: *Wren?*
She'd reply tomorrow. She'd reply when the world wasn't so loud with its silence, when she wasn't so drunk on the impossible fact of her own absence.
Tomorrow.
She fell asleep with her headphones on, playing nothing, the silence so complete it roared.
